learning_objectives: Design a performance measurement system for a project
Distinguish between leading and lagging indicators
Align project metrics with stakeholder information needs
readings: Kaplan & Norton, The Balanced Scorecard, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: You cannot control what you do not measure. Performance measurement provides the data for informed decision-making and early corrective action.
key_concepts: Performance measurement system: metrics, data collection, analysis, reporting, action
Leading indicators: predict future performance (e.g., requirements completion rate, risk closure rate)
Lagging indicators: measure past performance (e.g., actual cost, schedule variance, defect count)
KPI design: specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, time-bound metrics
Balanced measurement: measuring scope, schedule, cost, quality, and stakeholder satisfaction together
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a balanced set of KPIs for a software development project covering all five performance dimensions

review.value: Leading indicators enable proactive management; lagging indicators confirm what already happened; both are needed
Measuring only cost and schedule ignores quality and scope; all four constraints plus stakeholder satisfaction must be tracked

learning_objectives: Apply EVM to complex projects with multiple control accounts
Calculate and interpret EAC, ETC, VAC, and TCPI
Perform earned value trend analysis and forecasting
readings: Fleming & Koppelman, Earned Value Project Management, Ch. 7-10
PMI Practice Standard for Earned Value Management

lecture_notes.introduction: EVM at scale requires control accounts, work authorization, and disciplined data collection. Advanced analysis reveals trends that single-period data cannot.
key_concepts: Control accounts: WBS elements where performance is measured; the intersection of WBS and OBS
Earned value methods: 0/100, 50/50, percent complete, milestone weighted
ETC calculation methods: remaining budget, re-estimate, efficiency-adjusted
Cumulative CPI stability: after 20% completion, cumulative CPI rarely changes by more than 10%
Variance at Completion (VAC) = BAC - EAC: forecasted cost overrun or underrun
lecture_notes.demonstration: Multi-period EVM analysis of a project with 5 control accounts, including trend charts and forecasting

review.value: Research shows cumulative CPI stabilizes by 20% completion; early CPI is a reliable predictor of final cost performance
The level at which EVM data is collected and performance responsibility is assigned; typically 2-3 levels below the project level

learning_objectives: Apply velocity, cycle time, and throughput metrics in agile projects
Compare EVM and agile metrics for hybrid projects
readings: Cohn, Agile Estimating and Planning, Ch. 18-20

lecture_notes.introduction: Agile projects measure performance differently. Flow metrics replace earned value, and team health matters as much as throughput.
key_concepts: Velocity: story points per sprint; descriptive metric for forecasting, not a performance target
Cycle time: calendar time from work started to work done; measures flow efficiency
Throughput: number of items completed per time period; measures delivery rate
Cumulative flow diagram: visualizes WIP, throughput, and cycle time simultaneously
AgileEVM: mapping earned value concepts to agile (planned value = planned story points x sprint)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an agile performance dashboard with velocity, cycle time, and cumulative flow for a 6-sprint dataset

review.value: Use velocity for planning, never for performance evaluation; pressuring velocity leads to story point inflation
Maps PV to planned story points and EV to completed story points; enables earned value reporting for agile in enterprise contexts

class_schedule.topic: Integrated Change Control and Baseline Management
learning_objectives: Implement an integrated change control process
Manage scope, schedule, and cost baselines through controlled changes
Evaluate change requests for impact on all project constraints

lecture_notes.introduction: Change is inevitable. Integrated change control ensures every change is evaluated for its impact on all project constraints before approval.
key_concepts: Change control board (CCB): composition, authority, meeting cadence
Change request process: submit, log, analyze impact, approve/reject, implement, verify
Impact analysis: scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, and resource impacts of each change
Baseline updates: re-baselining after approved changes to maintain accurate performance measurement
Configuration management: tracking deliverable versions and their relationship to change requests
lecture_notes.demonstration: Processing three change requests through a simulated CCB meeting with impact analysis

review.value: Every change affects multiple constraints; approving a scope change without adjusting schedule and cost baselines creates phantom variances
After approved changes, update baselines so EVM compares actual performance against the current approved plan, not the original plan

learning_objectives: Design performance reports for different stakeholder audiences
Diagnose performance problems using root cause analysis
Implement corrective and preventive actions with follow-up

lecture_notes.introduction: Performance data is worthless without analysis, communication, and action. The PM must translate numbers into decisions.
key_concepts: Report types: status report (current state), progress report (change since last), forecast report (predicted future)
Audience tailoring: detail for the team, summary for executives, impact for stakeholders
Root cause analysis: 5 Whys, fishbone diagram, fault tree analysis
Corrective action: bring future performance back in line with the plan
Preventive action: avoid anticipated problems before they occur
lecture_notes.demonstration: Diagnosing the root cause of a 15% schedule variance using 5 Whys and developing a corrective action plan

review.value: Keep asking 'why' until you reach the root cause; surface symptoms (schedule delay) often have deeper causes (requirements churn, resource unavailability)
Corrective fixes a problem that occurred; preventive addresses a risk before it occurs; both should be tracked and verified

class_schedule.topic: Predictive Analytics and AI-Assisted Performance Management
learning_objectives: Apply statistical techniques to project performance data
Evaluate AI and machine learning applications for project forecasting
Design a continuous improvement cycle using performance data
readings: PMI Report on AI in Project Management

lecture_notes.introduction: Historical project data is a goldmine for prediction. Statistical analysis and AI can detect patterns humans miss.
key_concepts: Statistical forecasting: regression analysis on project performance data
Reference class forecasting: using data from similar past projects to calibrate estimates
Monte Carlo simulation for project outcomes: probability distributions for cost and schedule
AI applications: risk identification from project documents, automated anomaly detection in metrics
Continuous improvement: using post-project data to improve organizational estimation and planning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running a Monte Carlo simulation on project cost data and interpreting the probability distribution
setup_requirements: Monte Carlo simulation tool or Excel add-in

review.value: Flyvbjerg: compare your project to a reference class of similar completed projects; their actual performance is the best predictor of yours
Every completed project is data; organizations that analyze this data systematically improve their planning accuracy over time

questions.question: A project at month 10 (of 15) has: BAC = $500,000, PV = $350,000, EV = $300,000, AC = $380,000. Calculate CPI, SPI, EAC (CPI method), EAC (CPI x SPI method), VAC for the CPI method, and TCPI against BAC.

rubric.key: Correct VAC and TCPI with interpretation

questions.question: The TCPI of 1.667 from the previous question means the team must perform future work at 167% efficiency to meet the original budget. Explain what this means practically and what the PM should recommend to the sponsor.
questions.answer: A TCPI of 1.667 means for every $1 spent going forward, the team must produce $1.67 of value. Since the current CPI is only 0.789 ($0.79 of value per dollar), achieving 1.667 would require more than doubling current efficiency, which is virtually impossible. The PM should recommend: (1) Acknowledge that the original budget of $500K is unachievable, (2) Present the realistic EAC range ($634K-$676K) depending on whether schedule recovery occurs, (3) Propose options: descope to fit the budget, request additional funding, or cancel the project, (4) If continuing, reset the cost baseline to the approved EAC and measure future performance against the new baseline.

rubric.key: Correctly interprets TCPI as virtually unachievable
Compares TCPI to current CPI to demonstrate impossibility

questions.question: A Kanban team's cycle time has been trending upward over the last month: Week 1 average 3 days, Week 2 average 4 days, Week 3 average 5.5 days, Week 4 average 7 days. What does this indicate and what should the PM investigate?
questions.answer: Increasing cycle time indicates that items are taking longer to flow through the system. This could indicate: (1) Growing WIP: more items started but not finished, creating context-switching overhead, (2) Bottleneck formation: one stage of the workflow is becoming overloaded, (3) Increasing complexity: work items are getting larger or harder, (4) Resource reduction: team members leaving or being pulled to other work, (5) External dependencies: waiting for responses from other teams or vendors. The PM should investigate: check WIP levels against limits, examine the cumulative flow diagram for widening bands (bottleneck indicator), review item sizes for scope creep, and check team capacity. Immediate action: enforce WIP limits strictly and address any identified bottleneck.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies increasing cycle time trend as problematic

questions.question: A project has had its scope baseline changed three times in the first 5 months. Each change was properly approved through the CCB. However, the team feels demoralized because 'the goal posts keep moving.' How should the PM address this?
questions.answer: The PM should address both the process and the human impact: (1) Process: analyze why three scope changes were needed in 5 months; if requirements were poorly defined initially, invest in better requirements elicitation for remaining scope; if changes are driven by market/business conditions, consider switching to an agile approach where change is expected and managed incrementally. (2) Human impact: acknowledge the team's frustration directly; explain that approved changes are legitimate, not failures; communicate the rationale behind each change so the team understands why; celebrate the work that was done, even if the direction changed; ensure the team sees a stable path forward with the current baseline. (3) Governance: propose to the CCB that future changes include an impact assessment on team morale and delivery confidence, not just scope/schedule/cost.

major_assignments.title: Integrated Performance Measurement System Design
major_assignments.description: Design a complete performance measurement system for a project including KPIs, EVM implementation plan, agile metrics integration, change control process, and performance reporting framework.
objectives: Define a balanced set of KPIs covering all project constraints
Design an EVM implementation plan with control accounts and earned value methods
Integrate agile metrics for hybrid project measurement
Create a change control process and performance reporting framework
deliverables: Performance measurement plan with balanced KPIs (3-4 pages)
EVM implementation plan with control accounts and measurement methods (3-4 pages)
Agile metrics dashboard design with velocity, cycle time, and CFD (2-3 pages)
Change control process document with forms and procedures (2-3 pages)
Performance reporting framework with report templates for three audiences (3-4 pages)

criteria: KPIs cover scope, schedule, cost, quality, and stakeholder satisfaction
KPIs are specific, measurable, and actionable
Control accounts properly defined at appropriate WBS levels
Earned value measurement methods selected and justified for each control account
Reporting cadence and data collection process defined
Agile metrics correctly designed and interpreted
Integration with EVM for hybrid reporting addressed
Change control process is complete with forms, authority levels, and tracking
Report templates tailored to three distinct audiences
Reports translate data into decisions and recommendations
Complete change control and reporting framework

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on EVM, agile metrics, change control, and reporting

midterm_examination.description: EVM calculations, KPI design, and agile metrics

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all performance measurement topics

class_participation.description: Participation in EVM exercises, dashboard reviews, and change control simulations

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. EVM calculation exercises and CCB simulations require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: EVM exercises may be done in pairs. The performance measurement system assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. EVM calculations must show work, not just answers. Data fabrication is treated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Spreadsheet application with charting capability required. Monte Carlo simulation tool recommended. Power BI or similar dashboard tool helpful.
